data.title: Chevron: Prolonged Iran War A Catalyst
data.description:
Chevron Corporation (CVX) is set to be a major beneficiary of the recent escalation in the Middle East as the war between the U.S. and Israel on one side, and Iran on the other, is dragging on for more than a month now and seems unlikely to get resolved in the short term. With the U.S. government prepared to continue to attack the Middle Eastern country, it is also likely that petroleum prices (CL1:COM) will continue to surge in the weeks and months ahead... which creates a powerful earnings and free cash flow catalyst for Chevron and other major oil and gas companies.

I like the growth and investment setup here for Chevron, and while I recognize that the energy enterprise has already seen a significant increase in market cap since the war broke out, I believe Chevron is on track to post billions in additional earnings in 2026...if energy prices hold up. This gives the energy company potential for accelerating stock buybacks and could lead to a revaluation following the Q1'26 earnings report.

Previous rating

I rated Chevron a Strong Buy in my last coverage on the oil and gas major -- Why More Upside Looms In 2026 -- because I liked the growth upside that was tied to investments in Guyana and the Permian Basin. Chevron has made strategic acquisitions in the Permian Basin in order to increase its net acreage in 2023 -- through the merger with PDC Energy -- which now benefits the company in terms of strong production growth rates. With significantly higher prices for petroleum now present in the market, Chevron is set to benefit handsomely in 2026 from higher earnings and free cash flows.

Iran war creates powerful earnings catalyst for Chevron

The U.S. and Israel teamed up for a major attack on Iran at the end of February, and this conflict is now officially in its fifth week, with no signs of a peaceful solution in sight. In response to the military attack, the Islamic Republic of Iran has threatened the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which approximately 20% of global oil supplies flow.

The Strait of Hormuz is a vital 21-mile-wide oil artery through which approximately 20-21M barrels of oil and a large portion of global LNG pass on a daily basis. Major shipping giants like Maersk (AMKBY) and Hapag-Lloyd (HPGLY) have already suspended transits through the Strait of Hormuz in a direct response to attacks on containerships.

As a result, fears over the security of oil supplies have heavily influenced investors, energy companies and petroleum markets... resulting in Brent crude oil prices (CO1:COM) reaching their highest price level since 2022, following Russia's invasion of the Ukraine.

Before the conflict began on February 28, Brent was trading at approximately $68 per barrel (the average price of the previous two months before the war started). Following the military attacks on Iran and the resulting blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, petroleum prices have soared to about $108 per barrel. Therefore, the weighted average Brent price for the first-quarter of 2026 now calculates to just about $79 per barrel (+$11 per barrel). According to Chevron's fourth-quarter disclosures, the energy company realized an average Brent price of $63.73 per barrel in the fourth-quarter.

Based off of Chevron’s price sensitivity table from a February investor update, a $1 per barrel increase in petroleum (Brent) prices is expected to lead to an earnings tailwind of approximately $600M on a full year after-tax basis.

Multiplying the the incremental price increase of $11 per barrel with the $150M (pro-rata) quarterly sensitivity figure leads to an estimate earnings gain for Chevron, purely related to the Iran war, of $1.7B for Q1 '26. With about 1.99B shares outstanding, Chevron could therefore add $0.85 per-share in earnings in the first-quarter, merely because of price impacts in the marketplace following the outbreak of a new war in the Middle East. As a result, I am expecting the oil and gas major to report up to $2.37 per-share in earnings for the first-quarter vs. a relative low consensus EPS of $1.98 per-share now. Because of this estimated profit windfall, Chevron is in a good position to accelerate its capital returns, especially its stock buybacks.

Chevron has been a prolific buyer of its own shares in the last five years, due to soaring crude oil prices in 2022 which kicked off a period of higher earnings and accelerating stock buybacks immediately after Russia's invasion of Ukraine.

In the last year alone, Chevron repurchased approximately $12.0B of its shares in the market, while Exxon Mobil (XOM) aka ExxonMobil spent a little more than $20.0B. Both companies have potential to increase their stock buyback plans in 2026 (and raise their dividends) if 2026 sees higher-than-average (+$70 per barrel) crude oil prices throughout the year.

Chevron’s valuation

Shares of Chevron have seen a major repricing in response to the recent outbreak of hostilities in the Middle East and are currently valued at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 18.7X. However, earnings estimates are also resetting higher and could continue to get revised to the upside as the Q1 '26 earnings date approaches: in the last 90 days, Chevron has seen a total of 16 EPS revisions of which 7 were to the upside and 9 were to the downside. The consensus non-GAAP EPS for the first-quarter currently sits at $1.98 per-share which, given the calculation provided above, seems to underestimate Chevron's Q1 '26 earnings potential.

Chevron is currently more expensive on a forward earnings basis than its bigger key rival in the U.S. energy market, ExxonMobil, which is due to Chevron growing its production base faster. ExxonMobil's shares are trading at a forward (FY 2027) P/E ratio of 16.4X and have generally traded in the same direction as those of Chevron in recent years.

I believe a ~20.0X P/E ratio could be justified for Chevron, under the condition that petroleum prices remain above $100 per barrel for the foreseeable future. This, of course, would ultimately depend on a continuation of hostilities in the Middle East and persistent uncertainty about oil and NGL flows out of the Strait of Hormuz. A ~20.0X FY 2027 P/E ratio for Chevron, using my Q1 '26 EPS estimate of $2.37 per-share on an annualized basis (+8.0% Y/Y earnings growth next year, according to SA), implies a fair value of $205 per-share.

Risks with Chevron

In the short and long term, the biggest risk for Chevron is a contraction in petroleum demand and, as a result, weakening average prices relating to its upstream business. In addition, Chevron is currently ramping up its production in Guyana and the Permian Basin, so a slowdown in these very important growth plays could be a negative for investors going forward as well. An imminent end to the Iran war also poses a significant risk for Chevron and its shareholders as in this case the market is likely going to see a normalization of petroleum prices, which in turn may lead to an earnings recession in Chevron's production business.

Final thoughts

Chevron is set to be major beneficiary of the recent escalation of the war in the Middle East and the higher petroleum prices that result from it. With growing supply fears and increasing turmoil in the Strait of Hormuz, a vital oil artery in the Middle East, chances are that petroleum prices will continue to go up as long as a peace agreement between the U.S. and Iran is not reached. Given that Chevron is growing its production footprint and that a short term solution to the conflict in the Middle East is not in sight right now, I believe shares remain very attractive for investors. Further, the energy enterprise benefits from positive EPS estimate revision momentum heading into earnings. With an estimated incremental net earnings impact of $1.7B for Q1 '26, Chevron could use its profit windfall for more stock buybacks and accelerate other growth-focused investments, such as in the Permian Basin.

data.title: Vertex Pharmaceuticals: Label Expansions For ALYFTREK And TRIKAFTA Plus Strong Demand
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Vertex Pharmaceuticals: JOURNAVX Prescription Demand And Povetacicept Developments Warrant "Strong Buy." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company was seeing strong demand in terms of its drug JOURNAVX [suzetrigine], approved to treat patients with moderate-to-severe acute pain. That, plus it was in the process of exploring the use of this drug in two ongoing phase 3 studies targeting patients with diabetic peripheral neuropathy [DPN] as well. It will continue to enroll patients for this program through 2026. For the 12 months ending December 31, 2025, it generated approximately $59.6 million in revenues from it. What I also went over was the fact that this company was in the process of advancing its BAFF/APRIL inhibitor povetacicept for the treatment of patients with Immunoglobulin A Nephropathy [IgAN] and Primary Membranous Neuropathy [PMN].

Speaking of which, as an update, it was noted that the company released positive results from a pre-specified 36-week interim analysis from the phase 3 RAINER study using povetacicept on top of standard of care [SOC] to treat these patients with IgAN. In essence, there was a statistically significant and clinically meaningful 49.8% urine protein to creatinine ratio [UPCR] reduction compared to placebo. This clinical effect was statistically significant with a p-value of p<0.0001. The point here is that the company already submitted several modules of its Biologics License Application [BLA] of povetacicept for the treatment of patients with IgAN. The expected completion for full submission was set by the end of March of 2026. The point here is that the company is vying for potential FDA Accelerated Approval of this BLA of this drug for these patients.

Even better, it is expected to use a priority review voucher [PRV] to speed up the review of it from 10 months to 6 months. I had a "Strong Buy" rating on this stock with all prior and the latest progress for this program. What I want to go over this time around would be the fact that it had achieved label expansions for both of its Cystic Fibrosis [CF] drugs ALYFTREK and TRIKAFTA. More about this below, but what I will also go over in the "Financials" section is the revenue growth it is achieving because of these CF drugs. That, plus to mention the fact that it has several other ongoing expansions that it is working on in terms of developing new medicines for the treatment of patients with CF. With the label expansion of TRIKAFTA and ALFYTREK in the United States for a new batch of CF patients, plus the win it just achieved in the development of povetacicept for the treatment of patients with IgAN, I believe that this stock should remain as a "Strong Buy" rating.

Newly Added Label Brings Forth CFTR Modulators To Patients That Weren't Eligible

One of the main things I wanted to go over for the purpose of this article is the new label expansions that the company just achieved for both of its Cystic Fibrosis drugs, ALYFTREK and TRIKAFTA. Before going over these label expansions, I believe it is important to briefly mention what this disorder is. Cystic Fibrosis [CF] is characterized as an inherited genetic disorder that causes thick, sticky mucus buildup in the lungs of a patient. Why is this a bad thing? That's because it leads to a host of other problems that these patients have to deal with, such as inflammation of the lungs, recurrent lung infections, and breathing issues. The lung damage that occurs can be so severe that it can lead to a patient's death. What happens is that the CFTR mutations that these patients have lead to the CFTR protein either being defective or a shortage of it. It is required to be at the surface of cells to control salt and water flow into and out of it effectively. Speaking of mutations, in order for a patient to be considered to have this disorder, they must inherit two defective [mutation] CFTR genes.

The company just had a huge win in terms of being able to expand its labels of both of its CF drugs. The thing is that beforehand, there had to be specific mutations in order for certain patients to be treated with ALYFTREK and/or TRIKAFTA. The new label expansions are those of treating CF patients with any variant that results in production of a CFTR protein. Why is this label expansion important? That's because CF patients that were ineligible before because of mutation status are now eligible to receive treatment with a CFTR modulator. It doesn't matter where the variant is located on the CFTR protein, and this new label now expands its patient pool immensely. This changes the label to be a more responsive approach to variants and the development of a CFTR protein. The new label expansions then are:

  • ALYFTREK [vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor] - treat CF patients ages 6 and older with a variant of the CFTR gene that is either responsive to it or produces a CFTR protein
  • TRIKAFTA [elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor and ivacaftor] treat CF patients ages 2 and older with a variant of the CFTR gene that is either responsive to it or produces a CFTR protein

The big win here is for the patients who are now eligible to be treated with CFTR modulators. There are now 800 more people in the United States who can benefit from either ALYFTREK and/or TRIKAFTA. The company has now expanded its coverage of its drugs in the United States to 95%. This expansion was possible thanks to expansion data where certain variants were tested either in the clinic or in vitro:

  • 564 variants responding to ALYFTREK
  • 521 variants responding to TRIKAFTA

This response data was a good move for the company to expand its label of its key drugs. As I will show below, its revenue growth was driven by strong CF demand during 2025, along with some help from JOURNAVX and CASGEVY.

The beauty of this company is to continue to innovate in developing new treatment options for patients with CF. To this end, it is in the process of developing two next-generation 3.0 corrector class drugs, which are VX-828 and VX-581. The first of which, that is more advanced in testing, the corrector VX-828, is being explored in a phase 1 proof-of-concept [POC] study of people who actually have CF. It is expected that enrollment and dosing of this trial will be completed in the 1st half of 2026. The other corrector, VX-581, is being explored in a phase 1 study in healthy volunteers. The point here is for the company to produce a superior next-generation corrector. The point of a corrector is to fix the structure of the CFTR protein [it is misfolded] and to also traffic it to the cell surface.

Another expansion opportunity exists that the company believes is important to work on, and this would be in terms of targeting all other CF patients who still just can't benefit from a CFTR modulator. In order to accomplish this, both Vertex and its partner Moderna (MRNA) are working on a CFTR messenger RNA [mRNA] modulator by the name of VX-522 to target 5,000 patients that don't benefit from CFTR modulation. The process of this program is that it is being evaluated in the multiple-ascending-dose [MAD] portion of a phase 1/2 study. Investors won't have to wait long to see whether this drug is effective or not, as there is an expected data release from this early-stage trial expected in the 2nd half of 2026.

Financials

According to the 8-K SEC Filing, Vertex Pharmaceuticals had cash, cash equivalents, and total marketable securities of $12.3 billion as of December 31, 2025. The truth is that this company is still doing well in terms of revenue growth, which is why I believe it is still a "Strong Buy." Consider that for its most recent Q4 of 2025 earnings report, it noted that Q4 of 2025 revenues came in at $3.19 billion, which ended up being a 10% year-over-year increase. The quarter itself was impressive, but not only that, it is still continuing to deliver on the revenue front when looking at the whole year as well. It reported full-year 2025 revenue to be $12 billion, which is a 9% increase from the prior year. The reason for the growth is thanks to continue growth from its CF franchise.

Speaking of which, it is continuing to make a move from shifting people from TRIKAFTA over to ALYFTREK [Vertex Pharmaceuticals Fourth Quarter and Full-year 2025 Financial Results, February 12, 2026, slide #5]. However, this is not going to happen immediately, and it is going to take a long time to do so. Even still, the company is not sitting around expecting such a move. With that being said, it is in the process of expanding TRIKAFTA this year in 2026. For instance, it completed a pivotal study exploring the use of this CFTR modulator in patients 12 months to < 24 months of age. Having achieved this, it is expected to file regulatory submissions to expand to this patient population of TRIKAFTA in the 1st half of 2026. With ALYFTREK, it is expected to expand its targeting of CF patients ages 2 to 5 with regulatory submissions during this same time period.

Plus, it is already evaluating the use of this drug in a phase 3 study to treat CF patients ages 1 to 2. I believe that its growth as a company is going well. Not just because of what it has achieved thus far, but also because of what its guidance is. It has given guidance that it expects its full-year 2026 total revenues to be between $12.95 and $13.1 billion. Of course the CF product portfolio is going to be a huge part of this, but even more so, it expects its other products, CASGEVY and JOURNAVX, to pick up the slack as well. How so? It believes that its non-CF-approved products could contribute on their own to more than $500 million in 2026. The earnings per share [EPS] of $5.03 in Q4 2025 [Vertex Pharmaceuticals Fourth Quarter and Full-year 2025 Financial Results, February 12, 2026, slide #13] was a miss in terms of analysts' expectations, but even so, not by much [it was a 2.3% miss].

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Vertex Pharmaceuticals. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the latest label expansion of both TRIKAFTA and ALYFTREK. The company now believes that it is going to be able to target about 800 more patients because of this label expansion happening. The risk here is that even though there is this pool of patients to go after, there is no assurance that it will be able to treat all of these additional patients. Plus, this new label expansion doesn't change the fact that both of these CFTR modulators have boxed warnings of drug-induced liver injury effects and/or liver failure. It is possible that sales expectations may not be met immediately with this new label expansion.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the other expansions being undertaken with TRIKAFTA and ALYFTREK. As I mentioned above, the goal is to continue to expand both of them towards other CF patient populations. For example, with TRIKAFTA, there is an expectation to submit regulatory applications to be able to expand the use of this drug for patients ages 12 to <24 months of age. The risk here is that there is no assurance that Vertex will be given approval for TRIKAFTA and/or ALYFTREK to treat such a younger group of CF patients. At the same time, another risk to consider would be the company's move that it continues to deploy. What is this? This would be in terms of slowly moving patients from TRIKAFTA over to ALYFTREK. Such a move to achieve this is not going to happen instantly, and this means that revenue could end up suffering a bit because of it. Thus far, this hasn't happened as the company continues to achieve revenue growth for both TRIKAFTA and ALYFTREK, but it is a risk that remains in place nonetheless.

The third and final risk to consider would be the positive results that the company obtained through a pre-specified 36-week interim analysis from the phase 3 RAINER study using povetacicept as a dual inhibitor of BAFF/APRIL to treat patients with IgAN. The reason why is because there is an intention to complete the full submission of the BLA to the FDA for this drug soon enough. First and foremost, there is no assurance that upon completion of the submission of this BLA, the FDA is going to accept it in its present form. Secondly, Vertex is seeking Accelerated Approval of it as well. There is no guarantee that the FDA is going to grant povetacicept U.S. marketing approval under the Accelerated Approval pathway based on achieving a statistically significant 49.8% UPCR reduction compared to placebo based on the pre-specified 36-week interim analysis. There is another risk item to consider here, and this would be in terms of maintaining approval, if given. What do I mean here? Well, the thing is that the phase 3 RAINER study is expected to continue to completion with two years of treatment. Furthermore, there is going to be the evaluation of the primary efficacy endpoint of estimated glomerular filtration rate [eGFR] slope through week 104. The point here is that should this final endpoint fail to be achieved in a statistically significant manner, then it is possible that the drug could be pulled off the market at such time.

Conclusion

Vertex Pharmaceuticals continues to do well in terms of its CF franchise of products. It is continuing to achieve yearly revenue growth because of its main CF product portfolio, along with having done well thus far with CASGEVY and JOURNAVX as well. Speaking of the CF franchise, ALYFTREK ended up being a huge contributor based on the demand it is achieving thus far. Total revenue in the United States is continuing to come in strong thanks to the demand of this CFTR modulator. In Q4 of 2025, U.S. revenues came in at $2.06 billion thanks to demand for the CF drugs.

This is important because this ends up being a 12% year-over-year growth. Even though Vertex has always relied on its CF drugs to do well as a company, what I believe is highly encouraging is that it expects its other products to do just as well. It has given guidance that it expects $500 million or more in revenues in 2026 to come from its non-CF products. Based on the ability for the company to continue to expand its labels of TRIKAFTA and ALYFTREK, plus the ability to expand beyond CASGEVY and JOURNAVX with povetacicept, I believe that I should maintain a "Strong Buy" rating on this stock.

data.title: Tilray Brands Is Looking Better
data.description:
I have followed Tilray Brands (TLRY) closely since it went public in 2018. I never really liked the stock until 2025. Most recently, I was negative on it again, sharing in early December that it was no longer a Strong Sell and rating it Hold. The company just reported its fiscal Q3 on April 1, and I am maintaining a Hold for now.

FY26 So Far for Tilray Brands

Tilray reported its fiscal Q3 on April 1, and it was no joke. The company headlined the "record results" it experienced with its revenue, but it was not so pretty. Overall revenue did increase sequentially to $206.7 million, which was up 11% from Q3 a year ago, but there were areas of strength and weakness. Distribution revenue grew 35% to $83.0 million. Cannabis revenue was the second-largest of the four segments, and net revenue rose 24% to $64.8 million. The Beverage business was quite weak, with net alcohol sales falling 24% to $42.6 million. The smallest business, Wellness (hemp products), grew 16% to $16.4 million.

Through three quarters of fiscal 2026, overall revenue has grown just 6%, with Distribution (European pharmaceuticals) at $242.3 million, up 23%. Cannabis, the second-largest at $196.9 million, has been up 9%. Beverages have declined 15% to $148.4 million. Wellness has increased 6% to $46.2 million.

The weakness in alcohol sales comes after several acquisitions, and, according to the 10-Q, consumers are drinking fewer drinks with alcohol. Tilray Brands does sell non-alcoholic beer and other beverages that have no alcohol. The strength in cannabis is due to market share gains in Canada and rapid international expansion. TLRY claims to be the largest provider of medical cannabis in Germany, and it operates in several medical cannabis markets. The gain in revenue in the Distribution business has come from a price increase (13% in Q3 and 4% year-to-date) and a volume increase (7% in Q3 and 11% year-to-date).

Tilray Brands provides gross margins for each of its four segments, and they range on a year-to-date basis from 12% (up from 11%) for Distribution to 38% for Cannabis (unchanged). Beverages have seen gross margin fall from 39% to 34% and were 32% in Q3. Overall gross margin for TLRY has been 27%, down from 29%.

The company reported adjusted EBITDA of $10.7 million in Q3, which was up 19% from a year earlier, and the year-to-date adjusted EBITDA has been up 7% to $29.3 million. The company reconfirmed its outlook for FY26, guiding to adjusted EBITDA of $62-$72 million. Ahead of the report, analysts were projecting that adjusted EBITDA would grow 10% to $60.3 million. The current outlook, according to Koyfin, is $60.0 million.

Cash flow continues to burden the company, with cash flow from operations during Q3 at -$21.9 million and at -$31.8 million year-to-date. The balance sheet remains in good shape, with the current ratio at 2.8X and net debt very low at less than $30 million. The company had $220 million in cash as of the end of February. Tangible book value fell during Q3 to $750 million.

Analysts, according to Seeking Alpha, currently expect FY27 revenue to grow 24% to $1.1 billion and then to increase 8% in FY28. Adjusted EBITDA, according to Koyfin, is expected to grow 28% to $76.5 million. For FY28, adjusted EBITDA is projected to soar to $112.2 million, but this would be a margin of 9.5%, up from 6.9% in FY26.

TLRY Valuation

The thing that stands out to me about the valuation of TLRY is that the stock closed right at its tangible book value. The balance sheet is much better than it used to be, and this low valuation relative to its tangible book value suggests limited downside potential. Here is a table that shows how it compares to the other five cannabis stocks on my Focus List:

There are a few Canadian LPs that trade below 1X, and they all have net cash. I added Aurora Cannabis (ACB) to my Focus List after the Q3 report in February, and hold a large position in my model portfolio. I also own a small position in Canopy Growth (CGC). The largest position I have is in Organigram (OGI), which trades at a slight premium to its tangible book value.

While P/TBV helps assess the downside risk in my view, investors care more about looking at these stocks relative to earnings. The enterprise value to projected adjusted EBITDA for calendar 2026 at 11.4X for TLRY is not so cheap. It stands out, though, relative to CGC.

As I think about where TLRY could get by the end of the year, I look at 10X as the appropriate ratio, but off of a blend of FY27 and FY28. I am using just 8% for the margin of adjusted EBITDA in FY28, which is below the consensus, and this works out to be $94.4 million. Using this lowered estimate, which is about $80.6 million for calendar 2027, it works out to $806 million enterprise value, and subtracting the small net debt leaves a market cap of $777 million, which is $6.25.

Tilray's Chart Looks Promising

The one-year chart for TLRY includes the market low in early April and then a lower price near the end of June. This was ahead of the reverse-split, and the stock soared over six-fold by October and is down a lot now, but still above the lows:

I liked that the stock held the low from Monday of $5.93 after it pulled back following the financial report, but the $6 area may not prove to be support. I see $5.50 and $5.00 as support levels that are well above that all-time low set in June.

Ahead of the report, I added TLRY back to my model portfolio at 420 Investor on Tuesday, paying $6.04. When it shot up after the report, I had wanted to trim it or exit it, but I did not do anything. I boosted it at $6.14 late in the day, leaving the position at about 6%, which is overweight relative to the Global Cannabis Stock Index that I strive to outperform. The model portfolio holds four names, and TLRY is tied for the smallest exposure, with the other two at 12% and 18.4% for a total exposure of $42.6% to Canadian LPs, well ahead of the index weight in that sub-sector of 29.1%.

Investors should compare the action of Tilray to peers. There are seven cannabis stocks in the Global Cannabis Stock Index, including TLRY, and here is how they have performed year-to-date:

All are down, but TLRY has dropped the most. Going back to the 2024 elections, these seven stocks have all fallen except for one, which I think still has some downside, and one that has increased 13%:

TLRY, down 65%, has declined more than all of the other Canadian LPs that are in the index except for one, Canopy Growth, and it has declined substantially more than the AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (MSOS).

Things to Watch Out For with Tilray Brands

I used to not like that Tilray Brands was so diversified, but my negativity has changed. Still, they have a lot going on, and there could be problems in each of their segments.

Tilray Brands has never proven itself to be a good acquirer of companies, something that their CEO and Chairman, Irwin Simon, did very well at Hain Celestial (HAIN), which he founded. Within cannabis, Hexo Cannabis proved to be a disaster, and the involvement with MedMen (convertible debt) proved to end very poorly. I have never understood the Aphria acquisition of CC Pharma GmbH in 2019, though perhaps there will be synergies with cannabis. The Wellness business is a brand I know well that Tilray bought in 2019, Manitoba Harvest, for which it paid C$419 million in cash and stock. This has been disappointing. Now, the company has been expanding rapidly with M&A in alcohol, buying craft brands. Most recently, it acquired a brand that was booming in the past but failing as a company, BrewDog. So, while it could present opportunities, M&A can introduce risks as well.

Another concern that I have about TLRY is its entry into the U.S. Hemp THC business. I don't believe that they have sold that many of these drinks, but they will become illegal in November due to legislation signed that ended the government shutdown. Of course, this could get cancelled, but so far there has been no action.

Weakness in the international cannabis market is another risk, as this is an area where TLRY has done very well. In Q3, international cannabis revenue soared 73% to $24.1 million, which was 37% of their overall cannabis revenue. In FY26, international has been 29% of cannabis revenue.

Conclusion

I like Tilray Brands enough again at this lower price to own some in my model portfolio, which is limited to a maximum of 20% cash. Coming into the day on Friday, I was at 15% cash. Despite the very beaten up sector, I am cautious on cannabis stocks, especially the MSOs. As I said above, TLRY is one of the four Canadian LPs that I own currently in that model portfolio, though it is not nearly as big as Organigram or Aurora Cannabis. Readers should interpret this as a trading call and not an investment call on Tilray Brands.

Of course, Tilray is more than just a cannabis stock. I discussed some of the risks above, and it may not do well, but I do like it near $6, as it is down a lot from all periods of time except for last summer, when it bottomed out at $3.51, an all-time low. Again, this occurred when the company was aggressively selling stock through its ATM shelf, but this is done. The share-count has been stable this year.

The widely followed stock on Seeking Alpha (139,840 followers), which has burned many investors over the years, is not loved by many right now. On the Seeking Alpha Ratings Summary page, it is quite clear that the analysts and SA authors don't love the stock, and the quantitative rating is a Hold as well:

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lumen Under Pressure As Turnaround Story Concludes (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I last wrote about Lumen Technologies, Inc. (LUMN) in September 2025 and issued a Buy rating when the stock was at $6. That was based on technical breakouts and the conclusion:

The future could be bright as Lumen approaches an inflection point when the legacy business slowdown will be eclipsed by new growth areas.

The February 2026 Lumen presentation provided a handy illustration of this inflection point and projected that new strategic areas will overtake legacy revenues this year.

That's clearly a good thing, but the other takeaway from the above visual is that top-line growth is projected to remain mostly flat as strategic revenues are forecast to grow only slightly faster than legacy revenues decline. The future looks assured, but is perhaps not as rosy as the 2025 rally priced in when it reached $11.95.

This article looks at why growth concerns are now eclipsing the turnaround story and why that is leading to a retracement in the stock. It also looks at where it would be a Strong Buy again.

Stage 1 Complete

I talked a bit about Lumen's history in my previous article, so I won't repeat myself too much. Essentially, it avoided Chapter 11 bankruptcy in late 2023 and 2024 by restructuring its unmanageable $20B debt pile. That took it back from the brink, but on horrible terms that it is only just overcoming by paying down debt and re-issuing it at new rates and maturities. The AT&T (T) deal in 2025 helped with this and allowed Lumen to focus on new sources of income.

This whole process can be thought of as the turnaround, and now that the inflection mentioned in the intro has been reached, it is mostly complete.

As CEO Katie Johnson said in February:

We’ve done what many thought we couldn’t – stabilized the business, strengthened our financial foundation, and earned credibility in the market. Now we’re accelerating from turnaround to growth.

So Lumen is no longer a turnaround story, and the focus will shift to growth. The problem is that there is not much top-line growth to look forward to in the coming years. Indeed, analysts are gloomier than Lumen in that regard and project contracting revenues.

The transition from turnaround to growth, or for that matter, growth to value, is often challenging for a company and leads to a period of depressed stock prices.

For a start, that transition rarely happens quickly.

Another reason is that the stock is no longer attractive to investors looking for a turnaround. Those investors may slowly exit and will need to be gradually replaced by growth-oriented investors. But again, where is the growth to attract them?

The stock may have to drop far enough to initially attract value investors.

Stage 2

Stage 2 looks to be underway and is typified by a retrace of the stage 1 rally. A 50-61% retrace is common, and LUMN has already retraced 50% and re-tested the daily 200SMA.

Zooming in slightly on the above chart, we can see the 200SMA led to a large bounce after February earnings. However, the rally faded, and LUMN tested the 200SMA again in March. The reaction there has weakened, and when you see a stock continually testing strong support and failing to move away, it tends to lead to a breakdown.

The current macro backdrop has not helped the situation, as higher oil prices could dampen demand, and central banks have shifted hawkish. For a debt-laden company like Lumen, that is bad news. I think the 50% retrace could break at some point in 2026 and that could lead to a slide towards the 61% retrace at $5. That could line up with the 200-week SMA.

Basically, the stock needs to get low enough to attract value investors. The valuation at $5 would be around 0.6 price to sales. EPS is negative so we don't have a P/E ratio to assess, but most valuation metrics already suggest LUMN is decent value at $7B market cap.

Stage 3

Stage 3 will unfold as the growth story starts to gain traction, but that may take years, and there would need to be upside surprises.

Lumen's revenues come primarily from its business segment, which accounts for $9.9B of the $12.4B total revenue. The business segment is further divided into 4 categories, and only one of them is growing (at 5%) according to the F&25 results.

That's not going to attract growth investors.

Furthermore, the long-term business outlook is still focusing on better margins and lower interest costs, while revenue growth is largely absent.

However, there is potential for this to change and Lumen talks about new opportunities such as East-West traffic. That is already an area growing at 13% CAGR and if hyperscalers keep hyperscaling, the demand for data transfers could be enormous.

Quite simply, we don't know how AI will develop, but we know the demands for physical fiber networks are large and likely to grow. Lumen is well-positioned to deliver through its existing network and could be the beneficiary of some upside surprises.

Conclusions

Lumen's turnaround stage is as good as complete, and the company has steadied. It is now attempting to transition to growth, but that process is a challenge and the stock price is retracing the transition stage rally. It has already dropped 50%, and will likely reach the 61% retrace later this year, around $5. That could be a good buying opportunity, and while strong growth is still distant, it would be attractive value and a good point to speculate for better growth in the future.

data.title: Target: Strategic No-Man's Land (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Target (TGT) has been one of the worst-performing retail stocks of its generation, severely trailing Walmart (WMT) and Costco (COST).

Besides a cheap valuation, I argued that Target can't offer investors an exciting story unless an activist investor-led push would completely change the company's strategic path.

Target's recently announced "Strategic Plan" is exactly what I, as an investor, didn't want to see. It's what I call a 'Let's do what's right and not do what's wrong' plan, and those rarely work.

I'm downgrading Target back to "Hold" for that reason.

Target Needs A Transformative Change

When people hear "Walmart," they know they're getting truly everyday low prices and a great e-commerce experience. When they think about "Costco," they can envision unique goods at crazy bargains, a surprising assortment, and amazing staff. When they think about "Target," I argue they can't really pinpoint a unique identity.

This is why, over the past decade, Target has generated investor returns that are minuscule compared to those retail juggernauts. Simply, Target has been losing customers at a fast clip, profitability has dropped, and revenue barely kept up with inflation.

Looking at these numbers, one thing is very clear: something has to change for Target to become an attractive investment story.

Target's Issue Is There's No Clear Way Out

Many popular brands went through a bad stretch. Not many of them came out stronger. Think about Nike (NKE), Under Armour (UA), Nestle (NSRGY), or Starbucks (SBUX), all of which experienced a sharp drop in customers, and none have so far been able to get out of the hole.

In my mind, the issue with such powerhouse brands is two-fold. One, customers who have churned are extremely tough to get back; Two, it's really difficult to truly change such expansive operations.

Let's say you used to shop at Target, but for one reason or another, you switched to Walmart, and found better prices, a larger assortment, and a reasonably fine shopping experience. Why would you go back to Target? Even if Target lowered prices, increased assortment, and improved the shopping experience, how would you learn about it? Even if you did, it would take quite some time before you're willing to give it another try.

This is the problem Target's new management is facing. However, here's the truth - Target isn't positioned to compete with Walmart or Amazon (AMZN) on prices; It isn't positioned to deal with them in e-commerce either, and; It's far from having the value proposition of a Costco.

The numbers speak for themselves, I think. Target consistently lags retail leaders and loses market share every quarter. It experienced eight negative comparable sales quarters during a period in which Costco and Walmart experienced none.

Consequently, operating margins have plummeted, and the recent fourth quarter was another step in the wrong direction.

'Do What's Right & Stop Doing What's Wrong' Strategy Rarely Works

So, with that extremely tall task on their hands, new management had to come up with a strategic plan to deal with all that, and they did.

Target plans to invest an incremental $2 billion in 2026, including more than $1 billion in additional capital expenditures and $1 billion in additional operating investments, all in service of accelerating growth

Plans include transforming in-store floor plans and displays, increasing payroll and training to elevate the guest experience, strengthening and evolving the assortment in key categories, and accelerating technology — including AI — to make shopping easier and more personalized.

And, they gave out their strategic priorities:

I'm sorry, but neither of these things is truly transformative in my opinion. I find it hard to believe that the previous management team didn't want to have a good assortment, elevate the guest experience, or implement technology.

This is what I call a no man's land strategy, which, for investors, can only be tested by actual results, rather than the ideas in the plan alone.

Expectations Are Now Demanding

Target is guiding for 2% growth in 2026, driven by a slight comparable sales increase and new store openings. They are also expecting a slight improvement in adjusted operating margins.

Wall Street is aligned with said guidance, expecting 2% growth in 2026, and a gradual acceleration after that:

Similarly, analysts expect improved margins starting in 2026 and further expansion in the following years:

Reminder, Target delivered three consecutive years of revenue declines and margin contraction. Last quarter was no better, and arguably worse, with a 1.5% revenue decline.

So, Target's guidance and consensus estimates reflect significant optimism on near-term change. In my mind, that is setting up investors for a possible letdown and negative catalysts of downward revisions in the coming quarters.

Valuation Is No Longer Close To The Floor

Concurrent with the increased enthusiasm, which I argue is too optimistic, the stock has experienced a significant re-rating.

At $120 per share, Target is up nearly 25% since my "Buy" article in December and over 40% from its November lows. As a result, the stock now trades around 15 times forward earnings, far from its 10x P/E floor:

Importantly, while a 15x P/E might appear low to some, considering Target is expected to grow earnings by about 5%, this reflects a 3x PEG ratio, only 1 tick below that of Walmart or Costco.

Personally, I find all of them overvalued, but I'd much rather put my money in the successful and proven giants than I would want to bet on Target's turnaround.

In my mind, closer to a 10x P/E would create a decent risk-reward setup, while the current 15x P/E creates extreme downside risks when combined with the possibility of downward revisions throughout the year.

Conclusion

After the activist investor news back in December, I thought Target was heading towards a major strategic change, possibly going the acquisition or merger route.

Instead, we got a strategic plan that I find non-transformative and lacking the necessary changes to ignite an attractive investment story.

With the stock up 45% from the lows, and trading at 3x PEG on optimistic estimates, I argue the downside risk is high, and downgrade Target to "Sell."

data.title: ROCY: JPMorgan Provides The Tax-Advantaged Return Of Capital Focused ETF
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

JPMorgan (JPM) launched the JPMorgan Equity Premium Yield ETF (ROCY) recently, a fund that is specifically focused on providing tax-advantaged distributions. As the ticker would suggest, they are looking at return of capital distributions. These are advantages because they defer tax obligations for investors. For the distributions that are classified as ROC, this defers tax obligations because it reduces the cost basis. Therefore, putting off the potential tax obligations until the sale of the shares.

One of my greatest hesitations for something like (JEPI) was specifically because of the exchange-linked notes (ELNs), which meant a call writing fund was producing ordinary income distributions. This can make it less appealing for investors who hold a lot of investments in a taxable account— which is admittedly a first-world problem. With ROCY, that is now not the case as they provide a tax-advantaged emphasis like most other call writing funds.

With new competition in the ETF space, this is a natural progression for JPM to provide, as there are a number of funds that now provide distributions that are heavily characterized as ROC to their investors. This also competes against the old school closed-end funds, primarily from Eaton Vance, which have also shown a considerable level of ROC distributions over the years.

ROCY Basics

  • Dividend Frequency: Monthly
  • Dividend Yield: TBD
  • Expense Ratio: 0.35%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $98.66 million
  • Structure: Active ETF

ROCY's investment objective is "to deliver current yield while maintaining prospects for capital appreciation and total return."

To achieve that and the key highlights, they provide several bullet points for investors:

  • Generates yield through a combination of selling call option spreads and investing in U.S. large cap stocks, seeking to deliver monthly distributions from associated option premiums and stock dividends, a portion of which may be return of capital
  • Constructs a long equity portfolio through a proprietary data science driven investment approach designed to drive portfolio allocations while maximizing risk-adjusted returns
  • Seeks to deliver less volatility than the U.S. large cap market

As always, no fund can guarantee the results, but the idea is to generate a substantial amount of ROC distributions.

The Fund’s strategy seeks to result in distributions that represent a returnof capital. To achieve this result, the Fund intends to use losses otherwise generated by the sale, disposition or termination of certain equities and/or options positions to offset the realized capital gains generated by the Fund’s equity investments and options overlay strategy. No guarantee canbe made regarding the tax characterization of the distributions.

The fund hasn't paid its first distribution yet, but it will be a monthly payer according to the dividend calendar schedule.

ROCY's Portfolio Construction

When it comes to the portfolio, it mirrors the S&P 500 Index. So the top ten holdings are all going to be the names that most investors are familiar with. Primarily, the heavy emphasis we've seen from the Magnificent 7 names that dominate the index. However, the fund lists only 110 holdings at the moment, so the mirroring is done in a significantly reduced number of total positions relative to the major index.

Given that being the tilt of the fund, it also makes sense that we see the information technology sector as the largest sector weighting, by a significant allocation.

With that, the actual more active part of the fund's strategy will involve its options writing. They'll be "selling and purchasing call options with exposure to the S&P 500 Index."

At the time of writing, ROCY has a total of four index equity options outstanding on SPY, 2 long call options and 2 short call options. They are laddered, with going about 1 and 2 weeks out currently.

This means they are using a call spread option strategy. This is a distinction from call writing funds that generally use short call positions. Overall, this still brings in some option premiums, though reduced by also buying the long calls. However, it also means that the fund can participate in further upside via the long calls, should the index start to rally sharply. Given the number of contracts and the strike prices, it also tells us that the fund is targeting a 100% overwrite of its notional value.

With all that said, since they are actually using option contracts here, unlike JEPI, this is where ROC distributions can come from. The potential gains can be offset by underlying losses. The end result can be unrealized appreciation, meaning that the NAV can stay flat or rise, while simultaneously realizing the option losses (or even underlying portfolio losses). Thus, the distribution then paid out to investors can get the characterization of return of capital, since the fund is tactically managing the portfolio specifically to produce realized losses.

This is precisely what Eaton Vance does with their tax-managed funds, such as the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Opportunities Fund (ETV). Though they'll also regularly have large portions of long-term capital gain distributions as well. Those are still tax-friendly at a lower relative rate to ordinary income rates, but you don't get the tax-deferred advantage.

To reiterate, in the prospectus of ROCY, they also leave the caveat open that: "No guarantee can be made regarding the tax characterization of the distributions." Though with the fund's specific strategy to target ROC, I think there is a good chance a relatively large portion should be classified as such.

Conclusion

ROCY is a newly launched ETF from JPMorgan that is worth paying attention to, given that it should provide tax-advantaged distributions to investors. This is primarily in the form of return of capital distributions. Distributions classified as ROC will reduce an investor's cost basis, therefore deferring the potential tax obligation until the time that the position is sold. For income investors familiar with Eaton Vance CEFs or a number of other call-writing ETF options that have launched over the last year, they will be quite familiar with this tax-friendly concept. Now, we have a fund that is being set up to specifically target this strategy that is available.

data.title: MSC Industrial: Increasingly Reliant On An Unproven Self-Help Story
data.description:
The U.S. manufacturing economy remains challenging. While the last three ISM Manufacturing reports have been above 52 (indicating expansion), hiring isn’t expanding, cost pressures are significant, and a general note of caution persists. That makes for still-challenging conditions for industrial distributors like MSC Industrial (MSM), Fastenal (FAST), and Grainger (GWW), even though the market has already moved ahead of a real recovery.

MSC shares are up about 10% since my last update, underperforming Fastenal and Grainger modestly (they were moving roughly equally until a post-earnings sell-off at MSC), but outperforming the broader industrial space by around five points.

At this point I’m more cautious on MSC Industrial shares. I think the market repositioning cycle has largely played out, and while I do still see some upside on pricing, the overall pace of the manufacturing recovery is still wobbly. What’s more, I think the story is transitioning to a place where MSC’s company-specific self-help initiatives are more critical to driving outperformance, and with years of unfulfilled promises here (albeit under different management), I think some degree of caution is warranted.

A Disappointing Mid-Year Report

While MSC Industrial’s fiscal first quarter results did meet my expectations of growth at the high end of the guidance range (up about 4%), the fiscal second quarter marked a step back with weaker results, partially offset by better guidance for the third quarter.

Second quarter revenue rose about 3% (down 5% sequentially), with price figuring even more significantly into the growth, as price drove 6.6% growth versus 4.2% in the prior quarter. With that, then, volumes did weaken noticeably, falling 4% after a modest decline in Q1.

The overall sales figure was about 2% weaker than the Street expected. Flat sales in National accounts remains a little curious to me given large recent manufacturing company reports, but the 6% growth in Core accounts is encouraging vis-a-vis MSC’s efforts to really strengthen or solidify their position with the small/mid-sized businesses that make up their core customer base.

Gross margin improved 20bp from the prior year and 40bp sequentially, beating by about 20bp, as the company continues to benefit from pricing actions (including tariffs and surcharges for inputs like tungsten, a key input for tooling). Operating income rose almost 9% on an adjusted basis, with operating margin up 40bp yoy and down 90bp qoq, missing the Street by about 10bp.

Performance Is A Little Challenging To Benchmark

Making matters more challenging for investors, it’s tough to really benchmark MSC today relative to peers and broader underlying market trends.

On one hand, MSC has continued to lag Fastenal in terms of daily sales, with the last available comparison (February 2026) showing a 13.3% vs. 4.1% difference, but that has virtually always been the case. On the other hand, while MSC has recently lagged machinery industrial production, the company’s overall performance relative to very low overall industrial production growth isn’t bad.

February saw the Metalworking Index climb another three-plus points to 56.3, indicating meaningful expansion, with the Future Business Index jumping to 69.3. That’s encouraging, and it at least partially syncs with management guiding for a stronger third quarter than the Street expected (about 1% higher in absolute revenue and 40bp higher for operating margin), but that was also before the U.S. started a war with Iran.

I also think it’s worth monitoring trends in average revenue per unit in the in-plant and vending businesses. The former declined about 6% sequentially this quarter, while the latter declined about 1%, leading me to wonder if there was some work-down in inventories that impacted overall daily revenue trends.

All told, I think MSC’s results largely reflect what’s going on in the wider manufacturing sector today – a shaky “touch and go” recovery where orders are improving, but where companies seem reluctant to really commit to expansion/growth given ongoing uncertainties around a host of issues, including tariffs, interest rates, and inflation.

Self-Help Is Becoming More And More Important

More casual followers of MSC can be forgiven for losing track of all the various restructuring and self-help efforts that have been launched at MSC over the last decade. From expansions and contractions of the sales force to similar shifts in SKUs to customer-focused service offerings meant to help with product selection, MSC has been active in trying to improve the business and reverse a long-term downward trend in margins and modest outgrowth relative to underlying industrial production. Unfortunately, many of these efforts have produced little, and at times it feels as though the strategy is more about throwing anything at the wall and seeing what sticks. This is a problem, as it is increasingly challenging to stand out in the distribution space – customers have more price transparency than ever before, and things that used to set MSC apart (like phone, then online, ordering) are now commonplace.

To this end, management offered some metrics around supply chain and inventory management efficiency that were encouraging, including reductions in supply chain headcount and costs and improvements in inventory turns.

While supply chain and inventory efficiency are critical in distribution businesses, MSC needs to do more. Fastenal figured out long ago that a comprehensive, high-value service model was more sustainable and profitable, and they pursued it aggressively. Although MSC did follow Fastenal into areas like vending and in-plant inventory management, MSC’s business is still far more tied to transactional relationships with relatively low switching costs. Likewise, Fastenal has long been ahead of MSC in terms of operational and logistical efficiency.

I’m not suggesting that MSC has to try to become Fastenal to succeed (even if that were possible), but I do think they need to fundamentally rethink where and how customers derive value from their relationship with MSC. Fastenal identified points of pain like inventorying/restocking and stockout-driven downtime and came up with solutions to solve them. MSC did some of that once (when catalog/e-commerce orders saved customers inefficient trips to local stores), but they need to really go back to that core approach and restructure the business around solving/easing the new points of pain for their smaller/mid-sized manufacturing clients.

The Outlook

My initial expectations for 2026 were lower than Street averages, so not much has changed relatively speaking for my model apart from trimming back my revenue growth expectations. I’ve decided to lower my ’26 revenue growth number from about 5% to 3.5%, largely out of concern about the broader economy and pace of the recovery.

With the company doing a little better on margin drivers, I’m expecting a little more operating efficiency, so the revenue/volume revision isn’t really impacting margins meaningfully.

Longer term, I’m expecting over 4% revenue growth over the next three years on rebounding U.S. manufacturing, with long-term revenue growth closer to 3%. There’s some upside potential in increased reshoring of manufacturing to the U.S., as well as self-help, and there is still some short-term leverage to further price hikes in tooling (Kennametal (KMT), Iscar, and others have already announced hikes).

I’m expecting EBITDA margins in the mid-11%s this year, improving to around 13% over five years, but further improvement toward 15% is very much a “show me” story. Likewise with free cash flow conversion; I expect around 7% to 8% FCF margins, which is a modest decline from the trailing average, as I do expect ongoing pressure on margins.

Discounted free cash flow doesn’t really suggest meaningful undervaluation today (pointing to a mid-to-high single-digit annualized total expected return), though margin- and return-driven EBITDA gets me to a forward multiple of 11.5x and a fair value around $90.

The Bottom Line

I liked MSC in December as a play on increased market optimism regarding the manufacturing economy, and that thesis played out decently well – a 10% move isn’t huge, but over five months it’s not bad.

At this point, though, I’m not as bullish on the shares. Much of that is valuation-driven, as the shares are basically at my fair value estimate. There are worse things in the world than owning a good company trading at fair value, but I do believe that MSC’s upside is increasingly tied to finally executing on strategic self-help initiatives. With new management unproven in this respect, I see more execution risk, and I may look to sell off some or all of my position as better ideas emerge, though I do still directionally like this exposure to an eventual U.S. manufacturing recovery - even if that should take longer than previously expected.

data.title: SRLN: 7.7% Yield Remains Attractive, But Rate Cut Expectations Reduce Allocation Case
data.description:
The SPDR Blackstone Senior Loan ETF (SRLN) is an active fund focusing on having underlying exposure in senior secured loans. At present SRLN offers a dividend yield of roughly 7.7%. The fund benchmarks itself against the Morningstar LSTA US leveraged loan index at present. The fund states their subject market to be fairly inefficient and therefore their active management would offer value added proposition to the investors in deploying capital in these corporate fixed income securities.

SRLN is a fairly diversified fund and therefore, an investor desiring above-market floating-rate income can in general consider these funds. The loans are primarily floating-rate instruments and thus the fund remains in general less sensitive to interest rate changes, in either direction. The key alpha here, if any is attributable to credit exposure rather than duration play.

Fund Relationship with Interest Rates, Credit Exposure, and Performance Linkages

SRLN has been a fairly decent allocation for income seeking investors vs the treasury bonds. This was attributable to the late interest rate upcycle. However, the performance is rather moderate when thought about in isolation with a ten-year CAGR of ~4%-4.5%.

In addition, this happened to be the case when over the same period, similar corporate bond ETFs with a far greater exposure to duration-sensitive corporate fixed income, did not perform particularly well, while those with less sensitivity to interest rate changes such as SRLN or (HYG) performed better.

With reference to the benchmark, the fund has tracked it well. Considering the active nature of the fund, some might suggest this to be moderate; however, when taking into account the illiquid nature of the underlying markets and securities in which SRLN is exposed, the comparison is more or less neutral.

Funds like SRLN, by virtue of relatively shorter-tenure loans and floating rate options, are largely exposed to the credit risk of the issuer rather than broader market interest rate exposure. In other words, SRLN is primarily exposed to underlying fundamental developments of its securities, along with market perception of their credit quality. This makes it appealing for those investors who do not desire volatility due to external factors such as actions of the Fed, inflation trajectory, or similar variables impacting capital values.

The current SOFR for the fund stands at 3.7%, which might be considered reasonable at present, taking into account the credit profile of the portfolio, along with the highly diversified nature of the fund. Like mentioned earlier, the fund has fairly low exposure to duration.

This comes especially handy when market expectations shift and central banks tighten policy, as witnessed following the post quantitative easing phase in recent years, where rising rates adversely impact duration-sensitive assets. The meaningful increase in interest rates resulted in long-term bonds and preferred issues significantly underperforming, with many producing negative total returns. An investor in SRLN would have tended to benefit by having a partial hedge within fixed income, along with receiving regular income in a rising rate environment.

Data by YCharts

Since it is clear that low-duration funds such as SRLN provide a stronger hedge in rising interest rate environments and tend to outperform duration-heavy funds, this benefit is not costless. An investor would not benefit from falling interest rates, unlike most fixed income products.

Therefore, exposure to SRLN should be considered if there is a constructive view that interest rates will remain elevated. However, such funds continue to make sense for investors who do not want to make themselves vulnerable to interest rate-driven volatility in their capital. These investors, who prefer to stay detached from continuous market commentary around rates, inflation expectations, and macro developments, may benefit from exposure to low-duration funds like SRLN.

Forward Expectations

Treasury rates are at historically elevated levels, and there is significant pressure on the Federal Reserve to moderate rates. The market is expecting rate cuts in the near term as well. Looking through this lens; at least at this juncture, SRLN does not fit particularly well.

The current ~4.38% is at historically high levels for the time being. Current expectations largely suggest limited further elevation from these levels, while the probability of downward reversion in the near-to-medium term cannot be eliminated. Even in this volatile capital market environment, the Fed has recently clarified that inflation remains in check and further rate hikes are not needed at this stage, even with recent energy shocks. With very low expectations for further rate hikes, along with meaningful perception that sooner rather than later, the Fed would begin to cut interest rates, the funds which were duration heavy and suffered significant capital loss in the recent rate hiking cycle might rebound just as sharply when the rate cut kicks in. Thereby, if there is an option for similar credit exposure with more duration exposure, that might be appealing to investors at this stage.

With reference to SRLN, considering the current interest rate trajectory, the ‘hedge’ feature of the fund is less valuable, while the cost of hedging, i.e., reduced upside in a falling interest rate environment — dampens the appeal for a fresh allocation at this point.

Coming to credit spreads now, the spread for SRLN is relatively high with reference to few other high yield funds. With respect to interest rate sensitivities, I have already mentioned that, the current trajectory for a majority of investors might favor the funds who have similar credit spreads but more duration exposure. Now analyzing the credit profile in isolation, while the fund is largely exposed to single B rated credit securities, the prevailing spreads might still appear reasonable in the current environment. However, it would also do good to note that the current credit spread is more of a late cycle phase rather than a fair compensation of the risk.

The fund is at large exposed to single B-rated credit securities.

Historically, single-B credit tends to command spreads in the range of 400–600 bps to adequately compensate for default and refinancing risks. The current levels, therefore, suggest that investors are being compensated for benign credit conditions, with limited margin of safety should spreads revert back higher.

However, at the same time, since overall loan tenure is moderate, future income might reset at higher rates, although that advantage is more gradual and is offset by immediate credit spread widening.

The fund although does not look altogether suboptimal even at present, but an investor might be better off checking in other income options carefully before committing capital to this ETF. The current SEC yield stands at 6.48%, which again does not reflect any weakness in the current distribution yield, but also not particularly compelling at the same time, when taking into account both interest rate and credit spread trajectory for the time being.

Limitations to the hold thesis

Interest rate decline expectations have become more uncertain due to recent geopolitical developments. At the same time, investor exposure to underlying fundamental credit prospects, along with the neutralizing aspect of interest rate sensitivity, might still be appealing for some investors.

This is especially relevant for pure-play income-seeking investors who do not desire having an exposure for the purpose of price appreciation, but are more focused on the distribution of income. Excluding a fund like SRLN, which largely neutralizes interest rate sensitivity and has performed relatively well in a rising rate environment, may not entirely appeal to investors who do not wish to time markets and prefer a buy-and-hold approach.

In Summary

SRLN has been a strong vehicle for investors who allocated capital prior to the rate hike cycle. The fund has not only outperformed rate sensitive fixed income bonds and ETFs but has also provided decent absolute returns, also marginally outpacing inflation. However, considering a meaningful probability of a rate cut cycle along with current credit spread levels being at the lower end of the range, the appeal for a fresh allocation is reduced, and therefore the fund is rated a hold.

data.title: Duolingo Will Be Back On Track: Buy The Stock
data.description:
Losing ground in 2025

In February 2026, Duolingo (DUOL) dropped 20% post Q4-2025 earnings after revenue guidance tumbled to an astonishingly low growth of just 15-17% for the full year 2026. A huge drop from the 39% growth in 2025, which itself saw sequential growth decline each quarter, with MAUs dropping sequentially from 135.3Mn to 133.1Mn, in Q4-2025 and annual growth dropping to just 14.1%.

You can see the quarterly growth rates drop on several metrics.

MAUs dropped 2 out of 4 quarters, in Q2-2025 and Q4-2025. DAUs trickled down to single digits with just 2.4%, 5.9% and 4.4%, in 3 of the last quarters of the year.

Still interestingly enough, paid subscriber growth continued, beating the trend with 6.1% growth in Q4, and 5.5% growth in Q3. Yes, much slower, but then this is on much higher bases, and given how fast growth deteriorated at the top, it could have been a lot worse. The subscriber penetration of LT MAUs also inched higher to 9.2% at about the same pace throughout the year. As a result, paid subscribers kept on growing, the money hasn't stopped flowing - Duolingo closed 2025 with 12.2Mn subscribers - its highest ever.

As a whole, contrast that with 2024, when Duolingo could literally do nothing wrong. Perhaps one could have seen it as the end of the explosive, hockey stage growth phase.

Just a quarter back, analysts had forecast a consensus estimate of 22-23% growth for 2026 even after ominous warnings from management that suggested that revenue growth had been pushed to the back burner, to revive user growth. Management had seen the writing on the wall and was pivoting to focus on user growth, but the street responded late and held out for a mid-twenties’ growth.

This is what management said on the Q4 call on February 26th, 2026:

But the short-term implication is that this year will see slower bookings growth and lower profitability. We expect bookings growth of around 11% in 2026, compared to the nearly 20% we believe we could achieve if we operated like we have in past years. Additionally, because we will offer AI features to more users and have slightly higher marketing spend, we expect our Adjusted EBITDA margin will decrease to about 25%.

We expect 2026 DAU growth to be about 20%.

Not selling earlier with such declines, was clearly a mistake something investors have paid for quite dearly, yours truly included. Coming on the heels of a terrible third quarter, which itself saw user, bookings and revenue growth stall this one was a shock, but clearly, I had overestimated how deep the rot was.

I had bought and recommended buying Duolingo in June 2024 and then in August 2024, after which I sold 50% and then another 25% on the way down. I recommended buying it in April 2025, not realizing that the growth had slowed down tremendously. I added some shares last week at $100.

What is the best strategy for investors going forward?

How should we invest in 2026, now that we are facing a new paradigm - a slower growing company focused on user growth, but at a much more attractive valuation?

Do or die: I completely agree with the strategy to make the user experience better is a great one for the long-term - it’s not even the question of whether it’s a good or a bad strategy, clearly it is a question of survival now. Duolingo messed up very badly in chasing paid subscriptions in the face of a slowing user base, especially by reducing free features and forcing customers to buy paid subscriptions, which were not even up to the mark in terms of design, production and likability. Simply, it was huge turnoff, and it has wiped out about $10Bn of its market from the past year, bringing it down to just $4.6Bn

I believe that they have learnt from their mistakes and are making the effort to reverse course. I don't believe that it’s a dollar short and a day late, this is still a tiny $1Bn company with a lot of growth ahead.

Driving DAU growth

Duolingo is focusing on the customer and improving the user experience, which they hope will lead to DAU growth. Management has forecast a target of 200Mn DAUs by Dec 2028, implying almost 100% growth from the current DAU base of 52.7Mn.

I crunched the numbers to check test management's strategy was realistic and feasible and get a handle on what the operating metrics and revenue would look like in 2028.

Mercifully, DAUs still grew 30% YoY and paid subscribers 28.4% in 2025. Subscriber penetration of DAUs petered down to 23.1% in 2025, from a high of 25.8% in 2022, but ARPU (Average Revenue Per Paid Subscriber) grew 12% to $71.6, with a better product mix from the more expensive “Max” subscription tier.

Extrapolating this to 2026-2028, I dropped the DAU conversion ratio to subscribers to 22%, which dovetailed with management's expected DAU growth of 20% in 2026. Extrapolating it further with several scenarios and estimates, I realized that Duolingo really needs to convert just 19% and 18% of DAUs to get to 14%, 12% and 16% paid subscriber growth to take them to 18Mn paid subscribers by 2028, and grow revenues by 16%, 15% and 18% in the next three years. I took very modest ARPU growth rates of 3% each, bearing in mind that Duolingo is passing on freebees to its cheaper "Super" tier from its AI rich "Max" tier. In my opinion, these targets are very achievable and possibly conservative, and we could be in for some positive surprises in the next 3 years.

I back tested it another way using subscription growth form their two paid subscription products "Super" and "Max". Here are the results, and assumptions. As you can see, the two estimates have very similar total subscription revenues, and number of paid subscribers. The only area where this table is conservative is ARPU.

Assumptions: The "Super" subscriber percentage is based on the CEO's remarks on the earnings call, that there were 10x more subscribers on "Super" than on "Max".The pricing is based on annual subscription fees of $60 for "Super" and "$160" for "Max". But on a monthly basis the subscription fees are higher, $12.99 for "Super" and $29.99 for "Max". However, fees vary by country and to balance out lower fees abroad, I kept it standard at the annual pricing, which lowers the ARPU as compared to the 1st estimate.

Still the two estimates show us that Duolingo's targets are modest, and with the right number of incentives and improvements in the user experience for "Super" users, it can grow at these rates. I don't management is going to be wrong footed this time, and I appreciate the conservative outlook.

What are the strategies being adopted to get there?

Numbers aside, these are the steps Duolingo is taking to get to 100Mn DAUs by 2028.

Duolingo decided to improve these features to make its app more user friendly.

  1. Video Call with Lilly – Included in “Super”, previously it was only in “Max”
  2. Replaced “Hearts” with “Energy” - this was a pet peeve, which often stopped users in their tracks when they ran out of “Hearts”.
  3. Free users have 25 “Energy” tokens, and “Super” and “Max” users have unlimited “Energy”. Duolingo's entire success was built on streaks, and this was simply terrible planning and short-term thinking. This was absolutely necessary.
  4. Voice first exercises – started experimenting with it in March 2026 for “Super” users.
  5. Speaking adventures - mid 2026, for all users, including free users.
  6. Explain my answer – Free for all users from Jan 2026.

Chess: As of 2025, Duolingo had already reached 7Mn users in just a year. Its biggest competitor, market leader, and strategic partner, Chess.com had 200Mn registered users with about 20Mn games played daily. Many of these were funneled by Duolingo's chess course, which teaches users to play chess up to an intermediate level, with some special AI features available on "Max" and then directs them to compete on Chess.com. I believe the Chess course will be a sustainable, recurring business for Duolingo.

There is some indication that these tactics and overall strategy is working in Q1-2026, itself:

From the earnings call, CFO, Gilian Munson:

Looking at bookings. Quarter-to-date, as of last Friday, Q1 bookings growth was tracking above our Q1 guidance.However, it's important to remember that we are heading into a particularly tough compare in the first half with dead Duo, a price increase, particularly strong advertising bookings and the rollout of Energy.

Challenges and weaknesses

Churn and one time usage: The language learning business has an inbuilt churn; a student will complete only one course and never return. Duolingo has to and is focused on increasing courses in even more languages and pivoting to Math, Music and Chess to keep users for longer on the platform.

Artificial Intelligence: There will be a lot of casual and sporadic usage for language learning on artificial intelligence LLMs like ChatGPT and others for travel translation needs, overseas online meetings, where the need is transaction based and specific. For those more inclined to forge their own way without the structure of a Duolingo designed course, sure, AI will be a limited and minor headwind. It will also increase competition from smaller shops that will leverage AI for cheaper or free courses, but it will be nowhere near the scale or completeness of Duolingo.

Lower margins and profits: Duolingo has decided to forgo $50Mn bookings this year in its quest to increase DAUs, which is also going to take a toll on its margins and profits. This may be an expensive gamble and even as patient investors like me wait it out, a couple of bad quarterly misses could easily knock a lot out of its stock price.

The bull case for Duolingo:

To be sure, a market cap of $4.6Bn, which is just 4.5x 2026 sales, with $1Bn in cash is easily one of the biggest positives, but we don't want to fall under a value trap.

Here are several other reasons:

For those who need the structure of a tutored class and a habit forming streak with someone telling them what to do, Duolingo is a huge positive and mitigates the AI headwind from LLMs, who do it for free. And you can be sure that ChatGPT is not going to give you a certification for a job.

CEO, Luis Van Ahn from the Q4-2025 earnings call:

So basically, all of our courses that teach the top 9 languages that we teach, which, by the way, cover more than 90% of our daily active users will have content up to Duolingo score 129, which is a particularly important score because that's the score that you need in order to get a knowledge job in that language.

It provides English certifications. The Duolingo certificate for English (DET) is accepted in universities, and for other VISA requirements in several countries, including the U.S., Canada and U.K.

A lot of growth is coming back from the same user/family which means customer acquisition cost is low.

Duolingo is using a lot of AI for its own classes, over time the classes have gotten better, and a lot cheaper to produce, the cost of shifting video call to the cheaper "Super" tier is not that high.

Over time it could become university level education, which could lead to government support/federal funding and so on. They have the scale, network and flywheel to do so, and I believe that is the long-term goal.
